profile_88771_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Politics, eh? It's one of those things that feels like it's always in the background, a low hum of noise and debate, especially here in Uruguay. I'm 23, I've got my degree, but I'm working an unskilled job, just trying to make ends meet. It's a bit frustrating, to be honest. You go through all that effort, all those years studying, thinking it's going to open doors, and then you're still struggling, watching prices go up at the *supermercado* and rent eating up most of your paycheck.

So, do I care about politics? Yeah, I guess I have to. The decisions they make up in the Parliament, whether it's about the economy, jobs, public safety – it all trickles down and affects my life directly. It affects whether I can ever afford my own place, or if I'll still be living with my parents when I'm 30. My friends and I, we talk about it sometimes, about how things are, what the politicians are saying. There's a lot of cynicism, too, because you see the same promises made over and over again, especially around election time, and then not much really changes for people like us.

I wouldn't say I'm glued to the news every single day, analyzing every little bill or speech. I've got enough on my plate just getting through the week, working, trying to have some kind of social life. But I definitely pay attention when there's a big election, or when something major is happening that could impact my job or the economy. With my education, I feel like I *should* understand what's going on, even if I'm not passionate about all the political games. It's not like I'm completely disconnected; I know it matters. It's more of a necessary awareness than an all-consuming hobby.

profile_88774_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
One cannot live and work in this country, especially running a place like mine, without being deeply invested in politics. My livelihood, my land, the future of my children – it all depends on who is in power and what decisions they make. You see what happens with inflation, with export taxes, with regulations on what we can grow or how we sell it. A bad government, or even just an incompetent one, can wipe out years of hard work in a season.

I've seen enough governments come and go in my 61 years to know that you can't just ignore it. You have to pay attention to who's promising what, what their track record is, and how they might affect the agricultural sector. Will they protect property rights? Will they ensure the roads are good enough to get our products to market? What about security in the countryside? These aren't just abstract ideas; they directly impact my balance sheet, my employees, and the stability of my family's future. I discuss it with my neighbors, read the newspapers, listen to the radio. It's not always pleasant, but it's a necessity.

profile_88776_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Honestly, 'interested' is a tricky word for politics. It's not like watching football or listening to music, things you do for pleasure. For me, politics is more like... a necessity. You can't just ignore it, can you? Everything they decide up there, in Montevideo, in the Palacio Legislativo, it trickles down right to my pocket, to the price of bread, to the bus fare, to whether I'll have a decent pension when I stop working.

I've been working since I was a young man, always trying to make ends meet, provide for my family. I've seen governments come and go, some better than others, some just talking a lot and doing nothing. You learn to be a bit cynical, but you can't afford to be completely oblivious. If you don't pay attention, you'll be the first one caught off guard when they raise the taxes or cut some benefit.

So, I follow the news, I listen to the radio debates in the morning on my way to the factory. I talk about it with my workmates during lunch, sometimes argue a bit, sometimes just shake our heads. We discuss what the unions are saying, what the president is planning. It's not that I love politics, no, but I need to know what's going on because it affects my life, my family's life. It's about making sure I'm not left behind, trying to understand who's really looking out for people like me, the working folk. When it comes time to vote, I take it seriously. I try to weigh things up properly.

For people like us, working hard every day, trying to put food on the table, it sometimes feels like a world away. I'm not one to read all the papers or stay up late watching debates. I've got enough on my plate with my job, getting home, making dinner, making sure the kids are okay.

But then, you can't really ignore it, can you? The price of yerba, the bus fare, if there are jobs or not, if the hospital is running properly when someone gets sick... that all comes from what these politicians decide. So, even if I don't understand all the big words or the complicated economics, I pay attention. I listen to what people are saying at work, what my neighbors are complaining about, what they're talking about on the news while I'm doing the dishes.

When it's election time, you definitely feel it. Everyone's talking, trying to convince you. You have to think about who might make things a little easier, or at least not make them worse. I vote, of course. It's important to vote. But do I *love* politics? Do I spend my free time thinking about it? No, not really. It's more like... a necessary evil, something you have to keep an eye on because it affects *us*. You hope for the best, but you don't expect miracles. So, I'd say I'm interested enough to know what's happening that affects my life and my family, but it's not like a hobby.

profile_88915_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process: Ay, how could one not be interested in politics? I've lived through so much in this country, 77 years, mind you. I've seen democracies, dictatorships, economic booms, and deep crises. Everything, absolutely everything, that happens in our lives—from the price of bread to the quality of healthcare, my own pension, and the future opportunities for my grandchildren—is touched by politics. As someone who had the privilege of a good education and worked as a professional, I always felt it was my civic duty to stay informed. It's not just about voting every five years, no. It's about understanding the decisions that affect us daily, the debates in the Parliament, what our leaders are saying and doing. Sometimes it's tiring, seeing the same old promises or mistakes repeated, but ignoring it? That's not an option. We have to know what's happening to protect what we have and to push for a better future, especially for the young ones. How could I not be very interested? It's the pulse of our nation, and I'm part of it.

profile_88916_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics. To be honest, I've seen enough of it in my 53 years to know that it's mostly noise. They all promise the moon before the elections, and then once they're in power, it's the same old story. Prices go up, jobs are still tough to find, and you're still working just as hard, if not harder, to put food on the table. For people like me, the unskilled workers, the lower class, what really changes?

My interest in politics isn't about their fancy debates or who said what on the news. My interest is practical: can I afford the rent this month? Is the cost of living going to make my already tight budget impossible? Will there be work next week? Will the bus fare go up again? That's what really affects my life, and that's the only part of politics that really grabs my attention.

I do pay attention, of course. You can't just bury your head in the sand. You hear things on the radio, at the coffee stand, from the neighbors. You know who the president is, which party is making the most noise, and usually when they're planning something that's going to hit your pocket. And when it's time to vote, you go. It's your duty, even if you feel like you're just choosing the lesser of two evils sometimes. But to be "very interested"? No, that's for the people who have time to watch every show, read every newspaper, or believe that their arguing online actually changes anything. I'm too busy living my life, trying to survive. So, yeah, I follow it a bit, I know what's going on generally, because it affects me. But it's not a passion.

I need to know enough to understand what's going on, who to vote for, and how to plan for the next month. It's not a hobby for me, it's just... life. You have to be aware to survive, to make sure things don't get even worse. So, yes, I'm interested in how things affect us, but not in all the drama and fighting they do. Just the practical stuff.

No, I've got enough on my plate with work, trying to make ends meet, and just generally living life. Who has the time for all that talk?

But then again, you can't just ignore it, can you? Everything that happens in the Palacio Legislativo, it trickles down to us. The price of the bus, the cost of flour at the market, whether I can find a decent job that actually pays enough to get by, if the public health clinic is any good when my mother needs it... all of that depends on who's in charge and what decisions they make. It's not like I have the luxury of being completely uninterested. I have to know enough to understand what's coming, to have an opinion when my friends or family start talking about it, and most importantly, to vote for who I think will actually do something good for people like us, not just for themselves or the rich ones.

Most politicians, they're all the same, making promises they'll never keep just to get elected. It makes you cynical, honestly. But it's our country, our future. So, while I don't wake up excited to discuss economic policy, I pay attention when it matters, especially around election time. It’s a necessary evil, you could say. It's not a hobby, it's just... life.

profile_88932_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:

My pension, for example, is always on my mind, especially with how prices keep going up. And the doctors, the public services – they all depend on who's in charge and how they manage things.

As someone who always worked hard, in a service role, I've seen firsthand how policies affect people's daily lives, from the very top to the very bottom. For me, it's about stability. I've built a comfortable life, and I want to see that protected, not just for myself but for my family, my grandchildren. I want them to grow up in a peaceful, prosperous Uruguay. So, I pay attention. I read the newspaper, I listen to the radio while I have my maté, I discuss things with my friends and my children. But I always vote, and I always make sure I understand the main issues. It's important to be informed, to do your part as a citizen. I care about the country, and I care about how things affect our economy, our security, and our overall well-being. So, yes, I'm certainly not indifferent, but I'm also not going to lose sleep over every little political squabble.

Healthcare too. Getting an appointment at the public hospital can take forever. And medicines, they’re so expensive. We rely on the public system, you know.

So, am I interested? Well, I don’t go to political rallies, and I don’t spend hours reading all the party manifestos. But I watch the news on TV every evening, and I listen to the radio while I’m cooking. I talk about it with my neighbor, María, when we meet for mate. We complain about the politicians, yes, but we also talk about what they *should* be doing. I vote in every election, it’s my duty, even if sometimes I feel like it doesn’t make much difference. I want to know what’s happening because it affects my plate of food, my bills, my grandchildren’s future. It’s not a hobby, it’s just... life.

profile_88947_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's one of those things you can't really ignore, can you? I mean, it affects everything – what I pay for groceries, how safe I feel walking home at night, if my nieces get a good education, even the price of gasoline. I'm 42, I've seen enough governments come and go to know that their decisions really do make a difference, for better or worse.

I work hard, same as most people I know. I have my responsibilities, my bills to pay, my life to live. So I don't have time to be glued to every political debate or rally, that's for sure. But I definitely keep an eye on the news, especially when elections are coming up, or when there's a big announcement about taxes or public safety. I read the main headlines in *El País* or *La Diaria* online, maybe catch a bit of the news on TV while I'm making dinner. And of course, everyone talks about it at work or when we're having mate with friends. You can't escape it.

What really concerns me are the practical things: the cost of living, which always seems to be going up, and how that affects our family budget. The insecurity, that's a big one too. It feels like crime is always on people's minds. And then there's the quality of our public services – healthcare, education – we pay our taxes, so we expect things to work well.

I sometimes get frustrated with all the talk and the empty promises, or when you hear about corruption. It makes you feel like maybe it doesn't matter who's in charge. But deep down, I know it does. My vote counts, and it's important to choose wisely. I want what's best for Uruguay, for my future, for everyone's future. So, I pay attention, enough to be informed and to make my choices, but it's not like it's my whole life. It's a necessary part of being a citizen.

profile_88948_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Mira, la política es como el fútbol, todo el mundo habla de ella, te guste o no. Yo tengo 28 años, soy vendedor, tengo que laburar para pagar el alquiler y llegar a fin de mes. Pero a ver, ¿puedo ignorarla? Imposible. Si los precios suben, si la inseguridad empeora en el barrio, si hay problemas con la salud pública... todo eso viene de ahí, de las decisiones que toman los políticos. Así que, aunque me da un poco de pereza a veces, y estoy medio podrido de las promesas que nunca se cumplen, sí le presto atención. Escucho la radio cuando voy en la moto, leo los titulares en el celular, charlo con los compañeros en el laburo o con mis amigos en el bar. Veo quiénes son los principales, qué están diciendo de los temas importantes como la economía o la seguridad. No soy un militante, ni voy a marchas todos los días, pero tampoco estoy ajeno a lo que pasa. Cuando hay que votar, voto, claro que sí. Es un derecho y una obligación, ¿viste? Tenés que elegir el que te parezca menos malo, o el que creas que va a hacer algo.

Entonces, ¿muy interesado? No, no tanto como para vivir pegado a las noticias. ¿Nada interesado? Tampoco, eso es imposible viviendo en este país. Diría que estoy en un punto medio, lo suficiente para entender cómo me afecta y para tomar una decisión cuando tengo que hacerlo.

It's important to vote, even if sometimes it feels like choosing the lesser of two evils. My parents always said that. It's our right, our duty.

But am I *very* interested? Like, following every single debate, understanding every complicated economic report? No, I don't have the time or frankly, the patience for all that jargon. I get the main points from the news, from what people are saying. Enough to know who I'm voting for, enough to know if things are going downhill or picking up. So, yeah, I'm interested enough to care about how my life is going to be, how my family will manage. But it's not like a hobby.

profile_89027_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's one of those things you can't really escape, can you? Here in Uruguay, it feels like every other day there's a new debate about the cost of living, security, or problems with public transport. I work hard, a full day at the office, pushing papers, making sure everything is in order. When I get home, I'm tired. I want to put my feet up, maybe watch some TV, or chat with my mamá. I don't have the energy or the time to dive deep into every single news story or follow all the ins and outs of what the politicians are doing.

Honestly, sometimes it all just feels like a lot of noise, a lot of promises that don't always materialize. You see the same faces, hear the same arguments, and the problems we face, like inflation or crime in the neighborhood, seem to persist no matter who is in charge. It can be quite frustrating.

But then, I can't just ignore it, can I? The decisions they make in government directly impact my wallet, the quality of the public hospital if I ever need it, or how safe I feel walking home at night. My job, my future, even the price of the *pan* and *leche*—it's all connected to politics. I'll always make sure to vote, because that's important. It's our right, our duty, to choose who we think will represent us best, or at least, who will do the least harm.

So, I keep an eye on the headlines. I'll listen when my colleagues talk about it during coffee break, or when my dad rants about it after dinner. I know who the current president is, and the main parties, what they generally stand for. But am I going to rallies? Reading detailed political analyses? No, not really. I'm interested enough to know what's happening and how it affects my life and my family, but I'm not passionate about it as a hobby or anything like that. It's more of a necessary awareness.

Every peso they talk about in the budget, every law they pass in the parliament, it directly affects my pockets, my family, our future. Will the prices go up again this month? Will there be work for my son next year? Will the public health system be able to handle it if I get sick? These aren't abstract questions; they're daily worries.

I don't just vote and forget about it. I wake up, read the newspaper while having my mate, listen to the radio on the way to the workshop. We talk about it all day, with the guys, with my wife at dinner. You need to know who's making decisions, who's promising what, and who's actually delivering. There's a lot of talk, a lot of promises, and usually, it's us, the working people, who end up feeling the pinch or getting a raw deal. So, yeah, I pay close attention. It's about looking after yourself and your own, making sure you're not caught off guard by some new tax or regulation. It's essential.

profile_89092_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Politics, eh? It's like the weather, always there, always changing, and sometimes it really messes with your plans. At my age, you've seen enough governments come and go to know that they all promise the moon, and then most just deliver a few dust bunnies.

But you can't just ignore it, can you? It affects everything. The price of bread, how much I pay for the bus, if my pension will actually be enough to live on when I finally stop working. And the security in the neighborhood, that's a big one. You hear about crime, and you wonder what they're doing up there in Montevideo to fix things. My kids, they're grown, but you worry about their jobs, about the future for their own kids. So, yeah, I follow the news. I listen to the radio on the way to work, watch the evening news with my wife. We talk about it with the neighbors, with the guys at the *taller*. Everyone has an opinion, and usually, it's about how things could be better.

I wouldn't say I'm *very* interested. I'm not reading all the detailed reports or going to political meetings. I've got enough on my plate with work and trying to keep things running at home. It can be exhausting, honestly, listening to all the back and forth, the accusations. Sometimes it feels like they're just arguing for the sake of it, not really thinking about us, the common people. But I have to know enough to make an informed decision when it's time to vote, and to understand why things are the way they are. So, it's not a hobby, it's more of a necessity.

profile_89109_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Politics, eh? At my age, you've seen enough of it to know it's mostly talk. I've worked hard my whole life, since I was a young man, doing what I could. Semi-skilled, they call it. Meaning you're good with your hands but not exactly running the show. And let me tell you, the politicians, they're always running the show. They come and go, with their promises, their big plans. And for people like me, what changes? The price of bread still goes up, the pension is barely enough to keep you going, and getting a doctor's appointment takes forever.

I don't have the luxury of sitting around reading all the newspapers, studying every speech. My interest is practical. Does this government mean I can afford my medicine? Will my grandchildren have a chance at a better job than I did? Will the streets be safe at night? That's what I pay attention to.

I vote, of course. It's a duty, isn't it? To at least try and make things a bit better, or stop them from getting worse. I listen to the news when I'm eating dinner, or sometimes my neighbor, Don Carlos, he'll tell me what's happening. He's always more into it than I am. But 'interested'? It's not a hobby, like watching fútbol. It's more like... a necessity. You have to keep an eye on it, because whether you like it or not, it affects your wallet, your family, your life. But I don't get excited about it. It's mostly worry, or disappointment. So, I keep an ear out, but I don't delve deep. Enough to know what's going on, but not enough to lose sleep over it, not anymore.

profile_89110_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
*Ay, politics. It's a never-ending dance, isn't it? I've seen more presidents come and go than I have fingers and toes. From the time I was a young man, working the land, it's always been the same story. The men in the city, they make their promises, mostly around election time. They talk about the campo, about supporting the small farmer, about fair prices for our goods. And then, once they're in office, often enough, they forget about us until the next election.

My life is here, on the land. It's about the sheep, the crops, the weather, making sure there's enough to eat for my family. That's what truly matters. I listen to the radio, of course, to hear the news, especially if it affects prices, exports, or if there's some new regulation about the land or taxes. You have to keep an ear out, otherwise, you might get caught unprepared. But to sit there and follow every debate, every argument between them? I don't have the time or the patience for that. My day starts before the sun, and there's always work to be done.

Sometimes, what they decide up there in Montevideo makes my blood boil. They make rules that make no sense for someone who actually works the earth. It feels like they live in a different world. But then again, you can't just ignore it. It's our country, our future, and what they do *does* affect us, for better or worse. So I pay enough attention to know what's going on, enough to vote when the time comes, because that's our duty, isn't it? To choose who we think will do the least harm, or maybe even a little good. But I don't dwell on it. There are sheep to shear and fences to mend.

profile_89111_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Politics. *Uf*. It's like the weather, isn't it? You can't ignore it, because it affects everything you do, but you can't control it either, no matter how much you complain. My day starts early, before the sun is properly up, with the animals, with the land. That's my real world. The prices of wool, the cost of feed, whether there's enough rain, if my old bones can keep going – those are the things that are really on my mind, every single day.

Politicians, they live in Montevideo, mostly. They talk a lot, promise a lot, especially when elections are coming around. They say they care about the *campesinos*, about people like us. But then the prices for our produce stay low, and the cost of everything else goes up, and the roads to get our things to market are still dirt. It's always been like that, as long as I can remember. My father used to say, "They talk and talk, and we keep working."

But you can't be completely blind either. If they decide to raise taxes on my small piece of land, or if they change the rules for the pension, or if they decide something about the health clinics... well, then it affects my life directly. I listen to the radio sometimes, in the evening, after the work is done. I hear about what the president is saying, what the different parties are arguing about. I know who's in charge, and I know when it's time to go and vote. It's a duty, you know? My parents taught me that. You have to vote for the one who seems least likely to make things worse, or the one who might, just might, do something useful for the country, for people like us.

So, am I going to sit around all day discussing every little thing they say in the Parliament? No, I have work to do. Do I completely ignore it? No, because I can't afford to. It's like a distant rumbling storm, you pay attention enough to know if it's coming your way, but you don't spend all your time looking at the clouds. It's important, but it's not the center of my life.

profile_89133_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
My farm is my life, my family's future, and the livelihood of everyone who works here. How could I *not* be interested in politics? Every decision made in Montevideo, or even further afield in Mercosur, has a direct impact on what I do, how much I earn, and whether I can plan for the next harvest.

Take trade agreements, for example. If the government negotiates a new deal, it could open up new markets for our produce or, on the flip side, flood our local market with cheaper imports. Either way, it affects my bottom line significantly. Then there are the taxes – agricultural taxes, export duties, social security contributions for my workers. A slight change can make a huge difference to profitability.

Infrastructure is another big one. Getting our crops to market relies on good roads, efficient ports. If the government isn't investing in that, or if they're neglecting the rural areas, it makes everything harder and more expensive. And don't even get me started on labor laws; they dictate how I manage my team, which is a major operational cost and challenge.

Of course, there's also the broader economic climate. Inflation, interest rates, currency stability – these are things I have to constantly monitor because they affect our costs, our loans, and our ability to invest in new machinery or land. I need a stable environment to grow my business and protect my assets.

So, for me, politics isn't just about abstract ideas or who's arguing on TV. It's about practical consequences for my business, my employees, and my family's well-being. I need to be informed, to understand the different proposals, and to vote for candidates who I believe will best serve the interests of the agricultural sector and provide a stable, predictable environment for businesses like mine. It's an absolute necessity for me to pay close attention.

I'm 46, I've seen enough governments come and go to know that they all talk a big game, but for ordinary folks like me, working hard every day, not much really changes. Those things directly impact my wallet, how much I can save, or if I can even save anything at all these days. And crime, too. You worry about your kids going out, about the neighborhood. So I keep an ear out for what the politicians are saying about those issues.

I always vote. It's important, a civic duty, even if sometimes it feels like a bit of a lottery. You pick the one you think might be a bit better than the others, hoping they'll actually do something useful for the country, not just for themselves. But am I really diving deep into every single policy debate or following every parliamentary session? No, I don't have the time for that, and honestly, a lot of it seems like noise. I care about the big picture, the things that affect our daily lives, but I'm not an expert and I don't pretend to be.

profile_89421_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:

It's not like I have a choice, really.

And honestly, a lot of it just feels like noise, like politicians arguing amongst themselves, making promises they don't keep. It feels like they're talking to each other, not to people like me. They come around before elections, full of smiles and grand plans, and then once they're in power, everything goes back to how it was, or sometimes even worse. It's always the same.

So, I listen when people talk about it at the bus stop, or when my neighbor complains about the cost of things. I'll read the headlines if something really big is happening, something that's going to hit my pocket directly. And I always vote, because *hay que votar*, right? It's our right and our duty. But to be deeply *interested* in all the ins and outs, all the details and the constant fighting? Nah, it just makes me tired and sometimes a bit angry. It's more like I *have* to be aware than I genuinely *want* to be involved in all of it.

profile_89423_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Politics, eh? Well, at my age, 59, I've seen a lot come and go. Presidents, parties, promises... It all feels a bit like a merry-go-round sometimes. I work hard, always have. Sales isn't easy, especially these days with prices going up on everything – groceries, electricity, petrol, you name it. So, when they ask if I'm interested in politics, my first thought is, how can you not be? It affects everything. They talk about big numbers on the TV, but for me, it's about whether I can fix the old washing machine or if I have to put it off again.

I always vote, of course. It's my civic duty, something my father always insisted on. I read the papers, listen to the radio news in the morning, and sometimes watch the debates when there's an election coming. I try to keep up, to understand what's going on, because it impacts my wallet and my life. But I'm not one of those fanatics who gets all worked up about every single thing. I've got a job to do, a family to look after, and not enough time in the day to follow every little twist and turn.

I care about things getting better, for the country, for my kids' future. I care about a stable economy, about not being robbed on the street, about having a decent public health system. But "very interested" sounds like I'm glued to political shows all day, which I'm not. "Not at all" or "not very" interested just isn't true either, because I do pay attention and it affects me deeply. So, I'd say I'm interested enough to make an informed decision when it matters, to understand the general direction things are going, and to complain when they're going wrong. It's a practical interest, rooted in daily life.

It's not that I'm obsessed with it, or that I spend my evenings reading political manifestos. No, that's for other people. But I need to know enough to protect my interests, to understand why things are the way they are, and to make an informed choice when it's time to vote. It's a necessity, really, more than a hobby. I'm a working man, 65 now, and what these politicians decide hits people like me directly in the pocket. If they raise taxes too much, or if the economy goes bad, it's us who feel it first. So, I have to be informed enough to protect my family's future, even if it sometimes feels like shouting into the wind. It's not a passion, no, but it's a necessary part of life. You can't ignore it, even if you want to.

Politics... *uf*, it's a constant presence, isn't it? At 53, and after seeing so many governments come and go, you learn that you can't just stick your head in the sand. Every decision made up there, whether it's about inflation, taxes, or even just the price of a *milanesa*, it directly affects my wallet, my job, what I can put on the table for dinner. I work in sales, so I'm out there every day, talking to people, hearing their struggles, seeing how the economy impacts everyone. I need to know what's happening.

I might not be one of those who goes to every political rally or spends hours debating the finer points of policy, I just don't have the time or frankly, the energy for that anymore. Life is busy enough just trying to make ends meet and keep things stable. But I definitely pay attention. I listen to the news on the radio on my way to work, catch the headlines online, and talk about it with my colleagues and friends. You have to be informed, otherwise, you're just left scratching your head when prices go up or new regulations come in. My university education taught me to understand complex issues, and I try to apply that, but I'm also realistic. Politicians make a lot of promises, and often, it feels like we, the working people, get left behind. So, it's not a passionate hobby, it's more of a necessary vigilance. It impacts my life too much to ignore.

profile_89568_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly, sometimes I wish I could just bury my head in the sand and ignore it all. But how can I? Everything they decide, everything they argue about up in Montevideo, it directly impacts my everyday life. The price of *la nafta*, the cost of the groceries at the *supermercado*, the quality of the public health system, even the security of our neighborhoods—it all comes back to their decisions. I've worked hard, got my education, and I know how important it is for things to be run properly, with a clear head and a plan. My background in administration means I appreciate efficiency and logical thinking, which sometimes feels very far from what actually happens in politics.

I earn a lower middle-class salary, so every little change, every economic shift, hits my family directly. We're not rich enough to be unaffected, and we're not poor enough to be completely subsidized. We're in that middle ground where we rely heavily on public services and a stable economy. So, when they talk about taxes, or inflation, or education reform, I have to pay attention. It's not just some abstract discussion; it's about whether I can afford to send my kids to that extra class, or if we'll be able to manage a small family vacation this year, or if I'll be able to save enough for a comfortable retirement.

Sometimes I feel frustrated, even disillusioned, with the whole system. You see the same old arguments, the promises that don't always materialize, and the never-ending cycle of corruption scandals. It makes you wonder if anything ever truly changes for the better for people like us. But despite that frustration, I can't afford to be completely disengaged. I read the newspapers, I listen to the news on the radio while I'm cooking, and I discuss it with my colleagues during coffee breaks. It's not just a hobby; it's a necessity to understand the currents that are shaping our country and, by extension, my family's future. I have to know what's going on to protect my interests and make informed decisions, especially when it comes to voting.

Ay, politics. It's like the weather here in Montevideo, always changing, always affecting everything. When you've lived as long as I have, you see a lot of promises come and go. I remember the times when things were really tough, and how much it depended on who was in the Casa Rosada. So, you can't *not* pay attention, can you?

It's not that I spend all day reading about it or going to rallies, no. My life is busy enough with my family, making sure everything is in order, and trying to stretch my pension a little further each month. But I listen to the radio in the morning while I'm having my mate, and I watch the news in the evening. When I go to the fair, everyone's talking about the prices, the doctors, if it's safe for the children to play outside. All of that, it comes from the politicians, doesn't it?

Sometimes it makes you tired, all the arguments and the corruption you hear about. It feels like they're in their own world sometimes, far from ours. But then you remember that your vote matters, and you have to choose the least bad option, or the one that promises to keep things stable. My interest isn't a hobby, it's more like... a necessity. You have to keep an eye on things, for yourself, for the grandchildren. You can't just close your eyes and pretend it doesn't exist, because it always finds a way to affect your pocket, your health, your peace of mind.

profile_89649_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
At my age, 77 years, I've seen enough governments come and go to know that what they decide in Montevideo directly affects my land, my animals, and my family's livelihood. It's impossible to run a farm, to worry about the price of milk or wool, about the cost of feed, or about the state of the roads that connect us to the market, without paying attention to politics. Taxes, subsidies, the interest rates on a loan, even how much they pay for our produce – all of that comes down to what those men and women decide in the capital.

Sometimes it feels like they forget about us out here in the campo. They make promises, and then you see very little change. It can be tiring, all the talking and the back-and-forth, especially when you've got real work to do from sunup to sundown. But I can't afford to ignore it. I listen to the news on the radio, I discuss things with my neighbors when we go to town, and of course, I always vote. It's not that I'm obsessed with every detail, but I need to know what's happening so I can prepare for what might come, for my children and my grandchildren who also depend on this land. So yes, it's something I have to follow, even if it's not always pleasant.

profile_89650_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
How could I *not* be interested in politics at my age, living in a country like ours? It's not some abstract hobby; it's the air we breathe, the water we drink. Every single peso that comes into my pension, every single item on the grocery shelf, the quality of the public hospital where my neighbor was treated, the future of my grandchildren's education – all of it is tied directly to decisions made in the Palacio Legislativo or by the executive.

I've lived through enough cycles, enough governments, both good and bad, to know that indifference is a luxury we simply cannot afford. My postgraduate studies, they taught me how to read between the lines, to analyze policy, to understand the broader economic and social forces at play, not just the headlines. And my work, dealing with people, seeing the impact of decisions on their daily lives, it keeps me grounded. I understand the complexities, but I also see the very real human consequences.

It's not always an enjoyable interest, mind you. Sometimes it's frustrating, seeing the same mistakes, the posturing, the promises that never quite materialize. But to turn away, to say 'I'm not interested,' that would be to give up on the idea that things can be better, that we can demand accountability, that we can influence the direction of our society. It's a fundamental part of being a citizen, especially for someone who remembers times when we didn't have the freedom to even speak our minds about these things. So yes, it's a constant, sometimes weary, but always very present interest for me.

Una quiere lo mejor para Uruguay, que haya trabajo, que la gente esté segura, que la salud funcione.
Así que, no es una pasión, no es que viva para la política. Pero no puedo decir que no me interese en absoluto. Es parte de la vida, y como me afecta directamente, tengo que estar al tanto, aunque sea lo básico.

profile_89762_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Politics is like the air we breathe here in Uruguay. You can try to ignore it, but it's always there, shaping everything. As someone who's worked hard to get a good education and establish myself professionally, I know perfectly well that the decisions made in the Palacio Legislativo or by the President directly impact my life. My salary, the cost of the supermarket, the quality of public services my children use, the stability of the economy – it all comes back to what the politicians are doing, or not doing.

I'm not a fanatic, mind you. I don't go to every rally or spend all my evenings watching debates. I have a job, a family, responsibilities. But I read the news, from a few different sources, not just one. I discuss it with my colleagues during lunch, or with my brother over mate on the weekend. I try to understand the different viewpoints, because it's rarely as simple as one side says. My education taught me to look beyond the headlines.

I've seen enough governments come and go, enough economic ups and downs, to know that voting matters. It's not just a right, it's a responsibility. If you don't pay attention, if you don't engage, then you can't complain when things go wrong. I want what's best for my family, for my kids' future, and for the country. So, yes, I pay close attention. It's not always pleasant, listening to all the arguments and promises, but it's necessary.

profile_89766_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's one of those things you can't really escape, can you? As much as I'd sometimes like to just ignore it all and focus on my own life, it always finds a way to poke its head into our daily routine. I work in an office, so I see how everything from a new government decree to the latest budget discussion ends up affecting us, the regular people, whether it's our salaries, the prices at the *feria*, or even how many buses run in the morning.

I'm not one to spend hours watching political debates on TV or reading every newspaper article about the latest scandal. Who has the time for that after a full day of work and then trying to get dinner on the table and help the kids with their homework? My priority is making sure my family is taken care of, that we can pay the bills, and that we have enough food.

But that doesn't mean I'm completely oblivious. I do listen to the news when I'm making breakfast, or I'll catch the main headlines on the radio on my way to work. When they talk about inflation, or changes to healthcare, or what's happening with public safety, those are the things that grab my attention. Those are the things that directly affect *us*. I want to know enough to understand what's going on, and to make an informed decision when it comes time to vote. My parents always said it's our civic duty to at least know who we're putting in charge, even if sometimes it feels like they all make the same promises and then nothing really changes for us, the working class. So, "very interested"? No, not really. I have too much on my plate. But "not at all interested"? That's not right either, because it matters. It affects our pockets and our peace of mind.

I've seen a lot in my sixty years here in Uruguay. I lived through the *dictadura*, the struggle to get our democracy back, and all the governments since, from one side and the other. My postgraduate education taught me how to analyze, to dig deeper than the headlines. But it's my life, my work, and seeing what my neighbors go through that really grounds me. We rely on public services, on stable prices, on fair wages, and on a decent pension when we retire. All of that is decided in the political arena.

Of course, sometimes I get frustrated. Politicians can be a handful, full of promises that vanish like smoke. But even when I'm disillusioned, I can't afford to look away. It's too important. It's about our daily lives, our rights, and the future of our country. How could I not pay very close attention? It’s not just an interest; it’s a necessity to stay informed and understand what's happening.
